definition: The US is running 6-7% deficits at sub-4% unemployment, a combination with zero peacetime precedent in 74 annual observations (1947-2024). Net interest has nearly tripled in 5 years ($350B FY2020 to ~$1T FY2025-26). Every regression-based fiscal impact estimate is being applied outside its estimation sample.
evidence: 0/74 annual observations of this deficit-unemployment combination pre-2022 (census result, not statistical estimate)
Net interest nearly tripled: $350B FY2020 to ~$1T FY2025-26
Every multiplier estimate, deficit-to-term-premium regression, and Kalecki calibration is out-of-sample
Wartime precedent 1942-1951 exists but under fundamentally different structural conditions (Bretton Woods, capital controls, closed capital account)
open_questions: Does the wartime precedent (1942-1951) provide useful guidance despite structural differences?
How much should uncertainty bounds on downstream claims be widened?
Is AI productivity growth creating a structural break that makes historical comparisons even less valid?

definition: The Treasury basis trade ($800B-$1.5T notional at 20-100x leverage) is the single most concrete and dangerous amplification mechanism in the current financial system, creating extreme sensitivity to financing cost changes. March 2020 required $1.6T in emergency Fed purchases when current notional is 60-200% larger.
evidence: March 2020 required $1.6T emergency Fed purchases
Current notional 60-200% larger than March 2020
Leverage at 20-100x creates extreme financing cost sensitivity
All 4 debate pairs confirmed this as strongest cross-agent consensus
n=1 crystallization event means probability is fundamentally unquantifiable (Knightian uncertainty)
open_questions: What is the probability of unwind? (rated 2/10 confidence — fundamentally unquantifiable)
How does the SEC CMESC Q2 2026 clearing mandate interact with basis trade leverage?
Would Fed intervention be feasible at current notional sizes?
What are the cross-margining offsets that could dampen clearing mandate impact?

definition: A five-signal heuristic for staging fiscal dominance progression. The US currently scores 2.0-2.5 of 5, with Japan circa 2005-10 as the closest analogue. Useful as an organizing framework but NOT a validated predictive model — it is definitionally over-fitted (5 parameters on 4 data points, zero degrees of freedom).
evidence: Fitted to n=3-4 cases with zero degrees of freedom
Not falsifiable: no specified time horizon, no progression probability, no disconfirmation criteria
Signal 3 (implicit Treasury-Fed coordination) is inherently ambiguous
US at 2.0-2.5 agreed across pair_0 debate
Japan circa 2005-10 identified as closest current analogue
open_questions: Can Signal 3 (implicit coordination) be operationalized with observable criteria?
What time horizon should be specified for falsifiability?
What disconfirmation criteria would invalidate the framework?
Does 2.5/5 imply 50% progression probability? (Answer: no, explicitly rejected)

definition: The term premium is the primary market-based signal of fiscal dominance progression. ACM term premium swung from -50bp (2020) to +50-100bp (early 2026). The velocity of term premium change is more operationally useful than the absolute level for regime identification.
evidence: ACM term premium: -50bp (2020) to +50-100bp (early 2026)
Pre-QE regression: 20-40bp per 1pp deficit/GDP above ~3-4% structural threshold
UK Truss validated supply channel out-of-sample (~100bp in 4 days)
Clinton 1993 consolidation validated reverse direction (80-100bp compression)
Post-QE structural break is severe; QT-era sample (~3 years) too short for reliable re-estimation
open_questions: Where is the specific threshold level for term premium that signals regime shift?
How should the post-QE structural break be incorporated into estimation?
Is the QT-era sample sufficient for any reliable re-estimation?

definition: The speed of term premium change is more important than the level for distinguishing gradual from acute fiscal dominance pathways. >100bp/quarter indicates acute path; <50bp/quarter indicates gradual path. The US is currently on the gradual path.
evidence: Pair_0 resolution favored velocity over level framework
UK Truss episode: ~100bp in 4 days (acute)
Japan: gradual multi-decade term premium evolution
US currently below 50bp/quarter threshold
open_questions: What causes the transition from gradual to acute velocity?
Are there intermediate velocity ranges that are analytically meaningful?
Can velocity spikes be reliably distinguished from noise in real-time?

definition: The transition from early-stage to entrenched fiscal dominance follows a bimodal distribution: either gradual (10-25 years) or acute (months), with no historical moderate-paced transitions. The mode depends on institutional absorption capacity (domestic savings, regulatory capital direction, current account surplus).
evidence: 3 gradual cases: US 1942-51, France 1945-63, Japan 2001-present
2 acute cases: Italy 2011, UK 2022
0 moderate-paced cases
Institutional absorption capacity determines which mode
US has mixed features: reserve currency and deep markets (gradual) vs. current account deficit and basis trade amplification (acute)
open_questions: Can the US's mixed structural features result in a novel third mode?
What is the specific trigger that converts gradual to acute?
Does the basis trade amplification mechanism make the acute path more likely despite other gradual-path features?

definition: The most probable outcome of fiscal dominance for advanced economies is prolonged moderate inflation (3-6%) and financial repression producing persistently negative real bond returns — NOT crisis or hyperinflation. This 'boring middle scenario' generates the most significant wealth transfer from creditors to debtors over 10-20 years.
evidence: Systematic tabulation: 4/7 episodes produced moderate inflation (3-6%) with negative real returns
2/7 produced inflation de-anchoring above 10%
1/7 (Japan) coexisted with deflation
0/5 advanced economy, own-currency-issuer episodes resulted in default or hyperinflation
Pair_0 verdict: generalist_02 'decisively stronger' on this point
open_questions: Can the US sustain financial repression across 6-8 presidential election cycles under deep polarization?
Does the 'stealth' requirement for the boring middle scenario conflict with modern information transparency?
What is the cumulative wealth transfer magnitude under 10-20 years of 3-6% inflation?

definition: All probability estimates in the fiscal dominance literature suffer from a pervasive small-n problem that makes point estimates nearly uninformative. When the best evidence consists of single-digit counts, any implied precision better than ±20 percentage points is almost certainly overstated.
evidence: Fiscal dominance sequence: n=3
Consolidation success: n=13 (4 successes), 95% CI [9%, 61%]
Above-threshold term premium: n=7
Basis trade unwind: n=1
Reserve currency issuers with r≈g: n≈3
Current configuration: n=0
Confirmed in all 4 debates as meta-finding
open_questions: Is there a principled methodology for calibrating confidence in genuinely novel situations?
Should a mechanical confidence cap (e.g., 5-6/10) be applied to all extrapolative claims?
How should the distinction between factual claims and extrapolative claims be operationalized?

definition: A maturity-dependent correlation regime where 2Y-SPX correlation is negative (-0.15 to -0.30, reflecting Fed credibility) while 30Y-SPX correlation is positive (+0.05 to +0.15, reflecting fiscal supply dynamics). This bifurcation is a leading indicator of fiscal dominance. Falsifiable prediction: when 2Y-SPX correlation turns positive, the regime has fully shifted.
evidence: 2Y-SPX correlation: -0.15 to -0.30
30Y-SPX correlation: +0.05 to +0.15
Independently confirmed by two agents (generalist_01 and quant_researcher_02)
Pair_0 identified as 'specific, testable, and genuinely original claim'
Falsifiable prediction provides disconfirmation criteria
open_questions: Over what time window should the correlation be measured for regime identification?
What is the noise-to-signal ratio in real-time correlation tracking?
Does the bifurcation persist across different volatility regimes?

definition: The Kalecki-Levy framework establishes an accounting identity linking government deficits to corporate profits — this is irrefutable by construction. However, the causal interpretation (that the 6-7% deficit is the marginal driver of profit resilience) and specific threshold claims carry substantial uncertainty due to competing explanations.
evidence: The accounting identity holds by construction in every period
S&P 500 EPS growth of 8-10% through 2024-25 is consistent with Kalecki channel
Also consistent with AI capex, productivity gains, and buybacks (confounded)
Carry trap: the deficit that compresses credit spreads is the same deficit creating fiscal dominance risk
Creates negatively skewed payoff profile — carry narrows as risk rises
open_questions: What is the marginal contribution of the deficit vs. AI capex and buybacks to profit resilience?
How does the Kalecki channel interact with the carry trap under different fiscal scenarios?
At what deficit level does the carry trap payoff asymmetry become dangerous?

definition: The gap between the effective interest rate on federal debt and nominal GDP growth is mechanically tightening. Effective rate rose from ~1.5% (2021) to ~3.3% (2024) and is converging toward marginal rates of ~4.5-5.0%, while nominal GDP growth is ~5.5%. Cross-country evidence suggests 35-40% probability of fiscal stress within 10 years when r≈g.
evidence: Effective federal debt interest rate: ~1.5% (2021) → ~3.3% (2024) → converging toward ~4.5-5.0%
Nominal GDP growth ~5.5%
Cross-country panel (Mauro & Zhou 2021): 35-40% fiscal stress probability within 10 years at r≈g
Panel dominated by non-reserve-currency issuers
Reserve-currency adjustment essentially unidentified from data (n≈3)
open_questions: Does reserve currency status permanently modify or merely delay the threshold?
Is the bimodal outcome distribution at r≈g more informative than the 35-40% point probability?
Could AI productivity growth (0.5-1.0pp additional real GDP) invalidate the entire framework?

definition: The fiscal deficit that compresses credit spreads via the Kalecki profit channel is simultaneously the source of fiscal dominance tail risk. This creates a negatively skewed payoff profile where carry narrows as underlying risk rises — investors are being paid less to bear more risk.
evidence: Deficit sustains profits (Kalecki identity) → compresses credit spreads
Same deficit creates fiscal dominance risk → tail risk increases
Carry narrows while risk rises = negatively skewed payoff
Mechanism is logically coherent and follows from established framework
open_questions: At what point does the carry trap become a measurably bad risk-reward?
How should portfolio construction account for this asymmetry?
Is the carry trap already priced by sophisticated market participants?

definition: The 1942-1951 Treasury-Fed Accord period is the most relevant US historical precedent for fiscal dominance. The Fed capped long rates at 2.5%, debt/GDP peaked ~120%, inflation reached 20%+ during the Korean War. The period resolved by institutional negotiation (the Accord, March 1951), not crisis, followed by 35 years of successful deleveraging from 120% to 30% debt/GDP.
evidence: Full fiscal dominance: Fed capped long rates at 2.5%
Debt/GDP peaked ~120%
Inflation reached 20%+ during Korean War
Resolved by institutional negotiation, not crisis
Followed by 35 years of deleveraging from 120% to 30% debt/GDP
Political pressure to keep rates low when interest expense rises is structurally parallel
open_questions: How do Bretton Woods, capital controls, and closed capital accounts limit the analogy?
The 1946 US managed inherited debt; 2026 must manage growing debt AND growing interest — how material is this difference?
Does this precedent mean fiscal dominance is NOT a one-way ratchet?

definition: A self-reinforcing cycle where large deficits raise term premium, increasing interest costs, widening deficits further. The probability is approximately 20-30% over 5 years — materially lower than typically stated due to the conjunction fallacy (requires multiple links to hold simultaneously) and underweighted circuit-breakers.
evidence: Requires simultaneous: (a) large deficits, (b) significant term premium rise, (c) blended rate exceeds nominal GDP growth, (d) no credible consolidation, (e) Fed cannot intervene without inflation
Links are positively correlated, so joint probability exceeds independent product
Circuit-breakers: Treasury maturity management (30-50bp compression 2023-24), political response (1990, 1993, 1997, 2011 produced adjustment), denominator inflation
Fair estimate: 20-30% over 5 years, not 40-50% from naive 6/10 or 8-17% from independent links
open_questions: What are the specific conditions under which the doom loop activates vs. the dollar smile suppresses it?
How do circuit-breakers interact with each other?
Can the basis trade amplification convert the stagnation path into the crisis path?

definition: Fiscal dominance should be modeled as a conditioning variable (regime variable) rather than a standalone factor. Adding a 'fiscal dominance beta' to cross-sectional regressions would fail the Harvey-Liu-Zhu t-stat threshold of ~3.0. The correct approach is regime-conditional factor allocation because fiscal dominance affects all assets simultaneously as a systematic regime shift.
evidence: Harvey-Liu-Zhu t-stat threshold of ~3.0 would not be met by a fiscal dominance factor
Fiscal dominance affects all assets simultaneously (systematic regime shift)
Does not create cross-sectional dispersion suitable for factor modeling
Pair_2 validated as 'most methodologically sophisticated claim'
Adjusted from 9/10 to 8/10 as partly a restatement of existing methodology
open_questions: What observable variables should condition the regime identification?
How should portfolio allocation shift across identified regimes?
What is the estimation challenge with regime-conditional models given small-n?

definition: OPEC+ fiscal breakevens create a pro-cyclical amplifier that interacts with DM fiscal dominance. Saudi breakeven rising from $65-70/bbl (2019) to $85-96/bbl (2025-26) due to Vision 2030 capex. DM fiscal expansion sustains demand above breakeven (OPEC+ cohesion holds); DM fiscal consolidation weakens demand below breakeven (OPEC+ discord and supply volatility).
evidence: Saudi breakeven: $65-70/bbl (2019) to $85-96/bbl (2025-26)
Historical pattern across 4+ cycles: 1998, 2008, 2014-2016, 2020
Under Japan model: oil demand supported for years
Under acute Truss model: demand shock below breakeven triggers OPEC+ discord simultaneously with DM financial stress
Pair_1: 'strongest unique contribution' from commodities_analyst_01
open_questions: How does OPEC+ cohesion degrade as breakevens diverge across members?
What is the lag between DM fiscal consolidation and OPEC+ supply response?
Does the interaction amplify the gradual or acute path more?

definition: Under fiscal dominance, a major food price shock forces the Fed into an impossible trilemma: tighten (worsening the doom loop), accommodate (de-anchoring inflation expectations), or choose financial repression (formally selecting fiscal dominance). The transmission operates primarily through EM intermediation rather than directly on US fiscal dynamics.
evidence: Three confirmed historical episodes: 2007-08, 2010-11, 2021-22 (ag → EM fiscal stress channel)
Biofuel mandates (~36% of corn crop) create politically embedded demand floor
Pair_1 correction: not the MOST dangerous catalyst directly for US, but trilemma framing is analytically sound
Food: 30-50% of EM CPI baskets vs. 10-15% of DM
open_questions: How does the EM intermediation channel feed back to US fiscal dynamics?
Can the Fed credibly 'look through' food price shocks under fiscal dominance?
What is the interaction between biofuel mandates and farm-state political dynamics around debt ceiling?

definition: Fiscal dominance transmits through three distinct channels with different asset class sequencing: Channel A — financial repression (~50% for US, most likely), Channel B — acute crisis (~25%), Channel C — monetization (<5%). Each channel has distinct investment implications and historical precedents.
evidence: Channel A: US 1942-51, France 1945-63, Japan 2016-present
Channel B: Italy 2011, UK 2022
Channel C: Argentina, Weimar
Pair_0: 'most structurally useful contribution'
Probability assignments (50/25/5/20) are ordinal rankings from n=7, not reliable cardinal numbers
open_questions: What determines the transition between channels?
Can a country shift from Channel A to Channel B mid-episode?
Are the probability assignments (ordinal rankings) stable across different structural conditions?

definition: Gold at $2,800-3,000 is partially pricing sovereign/fiscal risk, with central bank buying at 1,037t (2023) and 1,045t (2024) as observable data. However, the $400-600/oz residual above traditional four-factor drivers competes with geopolitical diversification (post-sanctions), momentum, and model misspecification, making quantitative attribution irresolvable.
evidence: Gold $2,800-3,000 is expensive vs. traditional four-factor model
Central bank buying: 1,037t (2023), 1,045t (2024)
$400-600/oz residual has multiple competing explanations
Re-specifying with sanctions-era dummy absorbs $150-250 of residual
CB buying persistence: 5-8 year portfolio rebalancing toward 10-15% gold allocation
Gold models degrade 40-60% out-of-sample
open_questions: Can fiscal vs. geopolitical attribution ever be resolved?
Is central bank buying driven by fiscal concerns, sanctions risk, or portfolio rebalancing?
What is the 95% CI on fiscal attribution share? (estimated [15%, 85%])

definition: Fiscal consolidation has a historical success rate of approximately 30% (4/13 episodes), but the 95% confidence interval spans [9%, 61%], making the point estimate nearly uninformative. Three historically necessary conditions (monetary easing ≥200bp, FX depreciation ≥10-25%, gradualism) are largely unavailable to the US. The DOGE approach is structurally different from all 13 historical episodes.
evidence: 4/13 episodes succeeded, 95% CI [9%, 61%]
Three necessary conditions largely unavailable to US
DOGE structurally different from all historical cases
Belgium and Canada 1990s as excluded successful cases (survivorship bias)
Japan 1997 Hashimoto tax hike as analogue for DOGE-driven consolidation risk
open_questions: Does the DOGE approach qualify as 'consolidation' in the historical sense?
What is the probability of success without monetary/FX offsets?
Are Belgium and Canada 1990s genuinely comparable, or are structural differences too large?

definition: Fiscal dominance breaks the traditional demand-destruction thermostat across commodity classes. Under fiscal dominance, the Kalecki channel sustains household incomes, preventing self-correcting demand destruction from high prices. Commodities become endogenous to fiscal choices rather than exogenous shocks.
evidence: 2021-22: despite 40-year-high food inflation, food volumes declined only 2-3% vs. 8-12% in 2008 with smaller fiscal transfers
Kalecki channel sustains household incomes
Causation reversal: commodities become endogenous to fiscal choices under fiscal dominance
Represents structural change in commodity-inflation transmission
open_questions: At what deficit level does the thermostat fully break?
Does the mechanism operate differently across commodity classes (energy vs. food vs. metals)?
How does this interact with the Fed's 'look-through' doctrine?

definition: The SEC Treasury clearing mandate (CMESC Q2 2026) is a calendar-identifiable regulatory catalyst that could trigger basis trade deleveraging independent of any macro trigger. Central clearing increases margin requirements and counterparty transparency, both reducing leverage capacity.
evidence: SEC approved CME Securities Clearing Inc. December 2, 2025
Central clearing increases margin requirements and counterparty transparency
Historical parallel: post-2008 OTC derivatives clearing mandates compressed carry trades
Pair_3: 'most original and actionable insight' but flagged as under-examined
Could be phased in gradually or accompanied by cross-margining offsets
open_questions: Will phase-in be gradual enough to prevent disorderly deleveraging?
What cross-margining offsets might dampen the impact?
How does this interact with quarter-end repo stress as a compound risk?

definition: Under US-origin fiscal stress, both primary risk-off hedges (long UST and long USD) could fail simultaneously, eliminating the foundational assumption of most global multi-asset portfolios. No post-1971 US precedent exists, but UK 2022 validates the mechanism for a DM sovereign.
evidence: No post-1971 US precedent
UK 2022: sterling weakened alongside gilts
Would eliminate foundational assumption of global multi-asset portfolios
Appropriately low confidence across all agents and all debates
Logical contradiction with doom loop: doom loop predicts capital flight, dollar smile predicts capital inflows
open_questions: What conditions would trigger dollar smile bifurcation vs. the standard dollar smile?
Does the doom loop vs. dollar smile contradiction resolve via sequencing (smile holds in steady state, breaks at tipping point)?
How should portfolios be constructed to hedge this tail risk?

definition: The ON RRP collapsed from >$2T to near-zero, fundamentally altering the plumbing through which a fiscal shock would propagate. The Fed shifted to Reserve Management Purchases ($40B/month T-bills). Quarter-end SOFR spikes of 25bp above ON RRP signal non-crisis stress.
evidence: ON RRP collapsed from >$2T to near-zero
Fed shifted to Reserve Management Purchases ($40B/month T-bills)
Quarter-end SOFR spikes of 25bp above ON RRP
Dallas Fed: domestic banks are 'inelastic providers of marginal funding'
Only one agent develops this plumbing analysis — not corroborated
open_questions: Do SRF, discount window, and other facilities provide adequate substitution?
Is the plumbing change net positive or net negative for crisis resilience?
How does this interact with basis trade deleveraging mechanics?

definition: The Renewable Fuel Standard allocates ~36% of the US corn crop to ethanol, creating a ~$3.50-4.00/bushel corn price floor. Farm-state senators control marginal votes on debt ceiling legislation, making this politically embedded and resistant to fiscal consolidation. Creates a structural inflation ratchet under fiscal dominance.
evidence: RFS allocates ~36% of corn crop to ethanol
Farm-state senators control marginal debt ceiling votes
Creates ~$3.50-4.00/bushel corn floor
Pair_1: 'most underappreciated insight' from commodities_analyst_02
Political economy mechanism is precise and well-specified
open_questions: Could biofuel mandates be reformed under extreme fiscal pressure?
How does the corn floor interact with broader food price transmission?
What is the magnitude of the fiscal consolidation trap from SNAP/farm bill (~$120B/year)?

definition: AI productivity growth of 0.5-1.0pp additional real GDP growth could invalidate the entire r-g framework and prevent fiscal dominance activation. This could make large deficits self-funding if they finance productivity transformation rather than consumption. It represents the single biggest uncertainty that cannot be resolved by looking at historical base rates.
evidence: Mechanically improves r-g arithmetic
Could make large deficits self-funding if financing productivity transformation
No precedent in any estimation sample
Identified as 'single biggest uncertainty' by quant_researcher_01
open_questions: What is the probability of 0.5-1.0pp sustained productivity growth from AI?
Over what timeline would productivity gains materially alter fiscal dynamics?
Does the composition of deficit spending (productivity vs. consumption) matter for the outcome?

definition: China's commodity demand is shifting from iron ore (steel) to copper, with copper demand growing 4-6% annually while steel demand declines 2-3%. Grid investment requires 8-12kt copper per GW, EVs use 80-100kg copper vs. 20-25kg for ICE vehicles. China consumes ~55% of global copper. If China implements financial repression (YCC on CGBs), this rotation accelerates.
evidence: Chinese copper demand growing 4-6% annually, steel declining 2-3%
Grid investment: 8-12kt copper per GW
EVs: 80-100kg copper vs. 20-25kg ICE
China consumes ~55% of global copper
First time since 2012 that copper intensity is rising
open_questions: How does China's potential financial repression interact with US fiscal dominance?
What is the copper supply elasticity at current prices?
Is the rotation durable or dependent on specific policy programs?

definition: Exporter stocks-to-use (not global) is the relevant vulnerability metric for agricultural supply shocks. Global wheat stocks-to-use at ~32.7% looks comfortable, but major exporter stocks-to-use is only ~16%. Non-linear price response at low exporter stocks: 5% production shortfall triggers 30-50% price spike.
evidence: Global wheat stocks-to-use ~32.7% vs. exporter stocks-to-use ~16%
Non-linear price response: 5% shortfall → 30-50% price spike
One of the most robust empirical relationships in commodity markets
Not yet corroborated by other agents
open_questions: How do exporter stocks-to-use interact with trade policy under fiscal stress?
What is the threshold level for exporter stocks-to-use that triggers non-linear dynamics?
Are current stock levels trending toward or away from vulnerability?

definition: Japanese institutions hold ~$1.1T in US Treasuries. JGB yield increases of 100bp+ would sharpen repatriation incentives, creating a cross-border contagion channel to US term premium. Specific and testable but second-order relative to domestic dynamics.
evidence: Japanese institutions hold ~$1.1T in US Treasuries
JGB yield increases of 100bp+ would sharpen repatriation incentives
Second-order relative to domestic dynamics
open_questions: What JGB yield level triggers significant repatriation?
How quickly would repatriation flows impact US term premium?
Could the Fed or Treasury manage this with maturity management?

definition: The 1946-1980 US deleveraging decomposed approximately as: ~40% inflation, ~35% growth, ~15% primary surplus, ~10% financial repression. While based on well-documented data, the four-way split requires counterfactual assumptions, and the channels interact (inflation affects growth, primary balances are endogenous to interest rates). Modern replication is constrained by demographics and productivity gaps.
evidence: Based on documented 1946-1980 historical data
Four-channel decomposition: 40/35/15/10
Counterfactual assumptions create uncertainty in the split
Channels interact and are not independent
Modern constraints: labor force growth ~0.3% vs. ~1.5%, productivity gaps
Adjusted from 8/10 to 6-7/10 by pair_0
open_questions: How much do channel interactions affect the decomposition?
Is the modern demographic/productivity gap a binding constraint on replication?
What would a 2026 decomposition scenario look like given current constraints?

definition: Under fiscal dominance, both gold (monetary debasement) and copper (fiscal spending + weak dollar) should rally simultaneously, potentially breaking the traditional negative gold/copper correlation. 2020-21 and 2024-25 show early signs. A testable and actionable hypothesis that is not yet validated.
evidence: Under fiscal dominance, both gold and copper have reasons to rally simultaneously
2020-21 and 2024-25 show early signs of correlation breakdown
Testable and actionable hypothesis
Not yet validated
open_questions: Over what time horizon should the correlation be measured?
What other factors could cause the correlation to break?
Would this indicator lead or lag other fiscal dominance signals?

definition: No democracy has sustained intentional financial repression for >15 years without an external anchor or authoritarian governance. If the gradual repression path requires 25-35 years and must survive 6-8 presidential election cycles under deep polarization, political sustainability is questionable. Base rate: 0/0 (no comparable case exists).
evidence: No comparable historical case exists (base rate 0/0)
Gradual path would require surviving 6-8 presidential election cycles
Deep polarization complicates stealth repression
Could produce sawtooth deleveraging pattern through periodic political backlash
open_questions: Is 'stealth' repression possible in the modern information environment?
Would periodic political backlash produce a sawtooth pattern or full reversal?
Does polarization make repression harder or easier (divided government as de facto anchor)?
